data.title: The Mag 7 Hit A Critical Level
data.description:
With a sell-off in big tech stocks coming along with the current market rotation, we've seen the ratio of the Magnificent Seven stocks to the broader market slip. The average stock in the S&P 500 has outperformed the S&P 500 YTD, which is a rare occurrence in modern markets, which have largely been driven by these massive firms.

SA Quant Sours on the Mag 7

This was the first warning sign for me, as SA Quant has turned neutral on the entirety of the Mag 7.

Analysts are still largely bullish on these firms—bar Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA)—but SA Quant has downgraded the lot to a hold for now.

While it is unclear to me why SA Quant gives the ending grade that it does, since its weights are hidden to the public, we can see the ratings for each component.

What is clear is that Quant's largest problem with these stocks is their valuations. Most of them fail on this metric, with Microsoft (MSFT) scoring a D- and Amazon (AMZN) scoring a D+. That's still failing if we're going by university standards here. Not a good look.

These tech firms have been heavy for the indexes, as they contribute so much of the weight, accounting for up to 35% of the index among just those 7 stocks, while the rest is split among the other 493 stocks. This is complicated by their high valuations, which disproportionately weigh on the S&P 500.

The difference is stark to see when you put it like this: most of the stocks in the S&P 500 have lower forward price-to-earnings ratios than the index has by weight. Since these fast-growing tech giants own so much of the index by weight and have incredibly high expectations placed on them by the market—translating into high P/E ratios—they pull the index valuations up.

A re-rating here may be a good thing, especially if the bull market is to continue. So far this year, tech, communications, and discretionary firms (basically the sectors that encompass the whole Mag 7) have all seen valuation compression. Their prices are down, but their earnings per share are up!

The Mag 7 Hits Major Technical Support

That sell-off is now hitting a critical level that investors should be watching, not a fundamental level, but a technical one. The technical picture for the Mag 7 is easy to track—there's an ETF for that, the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS).

It just broke through its 200-day moving average and is approaching the anchored VWAP from its last deep lows in April, which should now create a strong support level.

Having broken through the 200-day moving average is damning evidence enough that investors are seriously worried about the Mag 7 and their prospects, trading down below that level of support. This next level is make or break for the firms; they need to hold the ~$60 level, or we should really watch out below.

If the fund falls below that mark, we can assume that there is no fundamental source of demand, and we could see a return to lows another 10% deeper, marking a bear market for this basket of stocks. Consider the stakes here to be fairly high, at least from a technical perspective.

So far, we've seen the fund respect that line, bouncing off it following the sell-off from earlier in the week. The lows seen on February 17th hit just above the $60 mark but recovered shortly after.

This marks what I believe to be a bottom for the Mag 7, at least in the short term (weeks). I expect the market to continue to respect that $60 level moving forward, and another rally could pull support up even further. That is something to watch for.

On the other hand, if I'm wrong here and the narrative around the Magnificent 7 being overvalued and needing a lot more correction in this ongoing market rotation is true, we stand to fall into bear territory from the last high.

If that happens, I would expect the rotation trade to light up even more. Expect outperformance of the S&P 500 by the Dow and by the Russell 2000.

The Market Still Cares About One Thing

I'm on the record for saying that my bullishness on the Mag 7 as a general basket and low marginal cost businesses in general (tech, comm services, etc.) is largely hinged on the thing that makes them special in the first place: profit margin expansion.

So long as this remains intact, I can see why now would be a local bottom for these firms. Here's a chart I keep coming back to on this point—there's been a clear correlation between forward profit margins and average valuations for the last two decades.

On this point, no matter how much pain the stocks have undergone this year, the Mag 7 really impress (except Tesla). Gross profit margins and net income margins are incredibly high; there's a good reason SA Quant gives all seven of them an A+ in this category.

Conclusion

The Magnificent 7 have seen their stock prices fall and valuations re-rated this year as part of the broader rotation that is currently ongoing in markets. The fall has now taken them to a crucial point, bouncing off the trend established in the last crash in April 2025.

For the MAGS ETF that tracks the Mag 7 stocks, this level sits at the $60 mark and is a pivot point for short-term trading. If the fund closes below that level, I would expect more bearishness to follow and for investors to need to “look out below” for these prolific stocks.

The long-term fundamental trends are still seemingly intact, with profitability (what I see as one of the key drivers of valuations in today's market) scoring excellent marks across the board with their margins still growing. This creates opportunity, as I see it.

I expect this to be a relative bottom for the Mag 7, which begets bullishness for the broad market, as they drive the larger trend in the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Alger International Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

During the fourth quarter of 2025, the MSCI ACWI ex USA rose 5.11% in U.S. dollar terms, with the Materials and Information Technology sectors outperforming the index, while the Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services sectors underperformed. Style factors such as size and value posted outsized returns, while liquidity and volatility factors lagged. Non-U.S. equity markets advanced, supported by the Federal Reserve's (Fed) well-telegraphed rate cuts in October and December, along with growing enthusiasm for artificial intelligence ('AI'), which boosted broader risk appetite.

European equities posted strong gains during the quarter, supported by outperformance in Financials as easing financial conditions and lower interest rates improved the outlook for lending and credit quality across the region. Resilient labor markets and moderating inflation further reinforced the Eurozone's macro backdrop, supporting investor confidence.

Emerging market equities rose in the quarter, supported by strong technology-led gains in South Korea and Taiwan and an easier U.S. policy backdrop as the Fed cut interest rates. Equities in South Korea advanced on surging demand for AI-related memory hardware, with additional support from Industrials amid a new U.S. trade deal that included tariff reductions and significant direct investment. In Taiwan, equities benefited from continued demand for AI-related semiconductors. Equities in China fell in U.S. dollar terms as concerns grew over softer economic data, ongoing property-market stress, and subdued investor sentiment.

In Japan, equities advanced as optimism around AI and higher defense spending supported investor sentiment. Additionally, Sanae Takaichi's election as prime minister and the formation of a coalition between the Liberal Democratic Party and the Japan Innovation Party were seen as signs of greater political stability and a higher likelihood of proactive fiscal support.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger International Opportunities Fund underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. The Alger International Opportunities Fund primarily seeks to derive performance from stock specific sources. Returns from individual stock selection detracted from relative performance, while factor exposures had a positive impact. The portfolio's overweighting of style factors such as medium-term momentum and exchange rate sensitivity were the most significant contributors to performance. On the other hand, the portfolio's underweight to dividend yield and overweight to value were the most significant detractors from performance during the quarter.

Among countries, South Korea, Italy, and Spain provided the largest contributions to relative performance, while Japan, the United Kingdom, and the Netherlands were the most notable detractors from relative performance.

Contributors to Performance

Heidelberg Materials AG (HLBZY), Hitachi, Ltd. (HTHIY), and SK hynix Inc. (HXSCL) were among the top relative contributors to performance.

  • Heidelberg Materials is a global building materials company that produces and supplies aggregates, cement, and ready-mixed concrete for construction projects worldwide, with a strong international presence and a focus on sustainable building solutions. Shares contributed positively during the quarter, driven by strong pricing discipline, margin expansion, and visible self-help cost savings that more than offset softer volumes in parts of Europe. A favorable regional mix—particularly higher-margin contributions from Africa, the Mediterranean, and Group Services—supported earnings resilience, while stable energy costs reduced volatility versus peers. In parallel, early monetization of its decarbonization leadership and premium products strengthened investor confidence into year-end.
  • Hitachi is a global industrial and technology company with leading positions in digital systems, energy, rail, and industrial automation, with an increasing emphasis on software-enabled solutions through its Lumada platform. We believe the investment case is supported by Hitachi's ongoing portfolio shift toward higher-margin digital and infrastructure businesses, improving returns on invested capital, and long-duration exposure to secular themes such as electrification, automation, and data-driven infrastructure spending. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after the company reported better-than-expected earnings, supported by strong demand across its digital solutions and energy infrastructure businesses, which led management to raise full-year fiscal 2025 guidance.
  • SK hynix is a leading memory manufacturer, producing DRAM and NAND flash used in smartphones, servers, and PCs. We believe the company is a leading supplier of high-bandwidth memory ('HBM'), a critical component in AI servers and accelerator systems. By focusing R&D on advanced HBM, SK hynix has gained share, improved average selling prices, and delivered stronger-than-expected revenue and margin growth as AI demand has accelerated. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as the company reported strong earnings growth that was significantly higher than consensus estimates, driven primarily by robust demand for its HBM products.
  • Xiaomi (XIACY) is one of China's leading consumer technology companies, selling smartphones and a broad suite of connected lifestyle products, while also expanding into internet services and smart electric vehicles (EVs). The company's "Human x Car x Home" strategy is designed to knit these offerings into a single ecosystem, using AI and smart connectivity to deepen user engagement and drive growth across its segments. Shares detracted during the quarter after delays in regulatory approval for a new EV manufacturing plant created uncertainty around production ramp timelines, while reduced government subsidies in China weighed on demand, contributing to slower-than-expected sales growth in smartphones and home appliances.
  • Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated "neocloud" beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the "circularity" of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Detractors from Performance

Nebius Group, Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals Co., Ltd. (JSHRY), and Xiaomi Corporation were among the top relative detractors from performance.

  • Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals is a China-based biopharmaceutical company that develops and commercializes prescription medicines. Historically, it has been best known for a large domestic portfolio in oncology and chronic disease, but the company is increasingly pivoting toward innovative drugs and a more globally oriented pipeline. We see this strategic transition being supported by expanding international clinical trials and a growing pace of partnering activity. During the quarter, shares detracted from performance after the company missed consensus earnings. This shortfall was primarily timing-related—driven by when an upfront licensing payment from GSK (GSK) was recognized—rather than a sign of deterioration in the underlying business. Licensing revenue can be lumpy and may swing results quarter-to-quarter even as the longer-term partnering trajectory remains intact.

Portfolio Positioning

We continue to position the portfolio to potentially benefit from secular trends including the following:

  • Energy security and transition, such as grid development, renewable energy, electric vehicles and batteries/materials
  • Digitization
  • Automation
  • Financial inclusion

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Real Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The economy likely slowed in the fourth quarter

U.S. gross domestic product ('GDP') growth was driven by robust household consumption and a narrowing trade deficit in the third quarter, but indicators suggested a slowdown in the fourth quarter. Survey data shows that the prolonged government shutdown dampened consumer confidence and spending. Recent trade balance improvements have supported GDP, but they likely reflect inventory accumulation from earlier in the year. As these inventories are drawn down, the trade balance should normalize, which could weigh on growth. Despite potential near-term weakness, we expect the economy to rebound in the second quarter of 2026 as fiscal and monetary stimulus begins reaching consumers and businesses.

Inflation trends lower, for now

Inflation, as measured by the U.S. Consumer Price Index ('CPI'), continued easing during the fourth quarter. However, the government shutdown caused disruptions in the data collection process that may have called its reliability into question. Looking ahead, core CPI is expected to remain below 3.0% over the coming quarters. That said, substantial monetary and fiscal stimulus entering the economy could spark a second wave of inflation later in the year.

The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) continues to cut

The recent softening in labor market data has allowed the Fed to continue lowering interest rates. We expect employment measurements, which are typically lagging indicators, to deteriorate further in the near term while core inflation remains below 3%. This could provide the Fed with justification for an additional rate cut in 2026. However, yields on U.S. 2-year Treasuries are roughly aligned with the effective federal funds

rate right now, suggesting that markets do not anticipate further easing unless economic conditions weaken materially.

Credit spreads remain tight

The economy likely slowed in the fourth quarter, but credit spreads remain near historical lows across both investment-grade and high-yield corporate bond markets. Demand for new issuance is strong, supported by attractive yield levels.

Although concerns around the scale of data center financing issues have drawn attention, overall issuer quality remains high. Investor appetite for these offerings continues to be robust.

Commodities, excluding oil, trade to new highs

Precious metals continued their upward trajectory during the quarter, with silver joining gold at new all-time highs. The rally broadened to include metals that are more sensitive to industrial demand. Platinum, palladium, aluminum, copper, and tin all posted significant gains. We believe this trend could signal an economic reacceleration in 2026, potentially followed by a second wave of inflation.

Fund Review

The Carillon Chartwell Real Income Fund (CCHIX) delivered strong relative and absolute performance in the fourth quarter. The Fund's I shares ranked in the 1st percentile of its Morningstar Inflation-Protected Bond peer group, and it was that group's top-performing fund for all of 2025.* The Fund's performance was supported by allocations to precious metals and equities in the materials and energy sectors. Although the portfolio's equity exposure is capped at 15%, its allocation was instrumental in smoothing returns in a rising-rate environment. The strong correlation between interest rates and cyclical sectors such as energy and materials proved highly beneficial.

During the quarter, headwinds to relative performance came from smaller equity holdings in the consumer discretionary, healthcare, and technology sectors. And while the Fund has maintained a shorter duration than its peers and the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ('TIPS') Index, it can hold some longer-duration TIPS to help hedge. These longer-duration bonds underperformed as the yield curve steepened during the quarter.

Top securities

Silver continued catching up to gold in the fourth quarter, and the Fund's position in the iShares Silver Trust exchange-traded fund ('ETF') rallied significantly. While we expect the price of silver to consolidate after its historic run, the precious metal recently eclipsed its previous all-time high, which was set in 1980 and revisited in 2011. We expect these past highs to act as a floor for the foreseeable future.

Investors are growing comfortable with the turnaround efforts at Teva Pharmaceuticals, accepting the company's path to earnings growth. Teva's management reiterated its peak sales target for its drug Austedo, and it has initiated two phase 3 studies for its Duvakitug product. We believe that Duvakitug, which has shown promising results in treating ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, has the potential to be a blockbuster across a handful of indications.

Taseko Mines continues to ramp up its Florence copper project in Arizona. This asset and its Gibraltar mine in British Columbia could produce a substantial amount of copper in 2026, with increased output in 2027. With copper trading at all-time highs, the company's cash flow could continue growing dramatically.

Birchcliff Energy is a natural gas exploration and production company with assets in the Montney formation of Alberta, Canada. The recent ramp up in exports from its liquid natural gas facility and cooler temperatures across the region have increased the demand for Canadian gas. Prices have rebounded from near-record lows, and we continue to view the Canadian natural gas market as an attractive hedge for global energy demand.

Intel rallied significantly during the quarter as partners started lining up for the company's semiconductor manufacturing facilities. Investments were led by the U.S. government, which has taken a significant stake in the company, and we believe that Intel's manufacturing facilities are becoming vital to U.S. national security interests. The United States needs to reduce its dependence on Taiwan's manufacturing complex, and Intel provides one of the better long-term solutions.

Bottom securities

Acadia Healthcare operates behavioral health centers across the United States. Over the past two years, it grew its bed capacity significantly and planned to continue expanding in the coming years. However, activist investors have recently pushed back on these plans, urging the company to concentrate on improving operations and ramping up recently built facilities, which often take at least 12 months to become operationally profitable. We agree with the activists' recommendations and believe that Acadia could turn cash flow positive in 2026, seeing significant operational improvement by the first half of 2027.

Camping World is a leader in the recreational vehicle ('RV') dealer space. The RV industry has faced affordability issues as higher interest rates and input costs, partially due to tariffs, have pushed average monthly payments higher for new RV purchases. We view Camping World as a consolidator in the industry that can benefit from smaller dealers facing increased operating pressures. The holding also acts as a partial hedge for an economy that may run above-trend growth while the Fed keeps short-term rates low. In such an environment, we would expect RV sales to pick up dramatically.

The press has documented Oracle's elevated capital spending on artificial intelligence ('AI') over the past year, but we feel the company's stock has finally reached levels that make it an interesting hedge against AI adoption. If AI usage were to ramp up significantly over the coming years, Oracle's investment in the infrastructure for housing large language models (LLMs) could create significant free cash flow. We do not believe the company's balance sheet is overextended, though we will continue to monitor whether its capital spending increases beyond the current 2027 projections.

Mosaic produces fertilizers and has a concentrated position of potash and phosphate operations in North America and Brazil. The potash assets have performed well, but the high-cost phosphate operations are still undergoing a turnaround effort led by a new management team. We believe this team will ultimately dispose of underperforming assets and improve the company's margins.

Coeur Mining is a gold and silver miner with operations in North America. Coeur recently proposed to acquire another gold mining company in an all-stock deal that is expected to close in the first half of 2026. The deal would be immediately accretive to the company's free cash flow, but some investors worry about the multiple paid for the assets given their shorter expected mine life. However, exploration efforts and higher precious metal prices could extend the operational profitability of the two acquired mines.

Outlook

The U.S. economy ended 2025 still recovering from the six-week U.S. government shutdown. Aside from the surge in capital spending on data centers, the fourth quarter showed few signs of growth. The housing market has stalled, unemployment is edging higher, and although inflation is trending lower, cumulative price increases since 2021 have left consumers facing affordability challenges for basic goods. Absent AI-related capital spending, it is conceivable that 2025 real GDP would have finished the year close to 1.0%, instead of near 2.0% as currently projected.

This slowdown will likely extend into early 2026, but there is genuine support on the horizon. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed last summer was designed to release a substantial amount of fiscal stimulus in the coming year. Consumers should see cumulative federal tax refunds rise roughly 45% year over year, injecting about $150 billion (roughly 0.5% of GDP) into the economy. Businesses may also benefit from changes to accelerated depreciation that are expected to total more than $200 billion. Lower interest rates and the Fed's resumption of asset purchases should also begin to filter through the economy, creating a more favorable environment for lending.

Taken together, these supports should strengthen and broaden year-over-year economic activity. However, the implications for inflation are less certain.

Recent CPI data has been muddied by the shutdown. The Bureau of Labor Statistics ('BLS') was closed in October; it did not collect survey data and could not publish an October CPI reading. November's data was affected by a shortened collection window. Even so, inflation pressures are clearly easing, and we expect that trend to continue into 2026. At the same time, the secular inflation forces we've highlighted in prior outlooks are likely to place a floor under how low inflation can go. If growth strengthens in the second and third quarters as we expect, a second wave of inflation could follow.

To assess near-term inflation pressures, it helps to unpack the CPI's key components. Core services account for more than 60% of CPI, and shelter is more than half of core services. Shelter is measured via tenant and landlord surveys on rents; it is less volatile than home prices and typically lags home-price turning points by about 18 months. Thus, even as housing softens, this large CPI component is unlikely to collapse.

The CPI's trailing six-month shelter measure is running near 2.5% (annualized), although October's collection issues likely depressed recent readings. Adjusting for those distortions, shelter inflation is likely below 3.0% with some room to drift lower. While there are pockets of oversupply in parts of the South, we believe shelter will find a floor above 2%, because national supply and demand remain relatively balanced. Historically, shelter inflation has only dipped below 2% during significant downturns.

Beyond shelter, core services are heavily influenced by wages. The largest subcomponents include transportation services (10.5% of core services), healthcare services (10.5%), education and communication services (8.1%), and recreation services (5.3%). Growth of average hourly earnings have recently fallen to a four-year low, near 3.5%. They may drift lower in the near term, because they typically follow the quits rate, which has reached a new low.

We expect wages to find a floor in the coming quarters, likely at or above 3.0%, before inflecting higher as the economy turns. With the working-age population shrinking relative to retirees, labor retains a secular tailwind in wage negotiations that could persist into the next decade. We view the current hiring freeze primarily as a response to economic uncertainty rather than as AI-driven displacement.

Turning to goods prices (18.3% of CPI), tariffs have been a notable driver. Household furnishings and new vehicles have contributed meaningfully to recent goods inflation, but their upward pressure should lessen as tariffs are annualized. However, as tariff effects wane, the costs for input commodities used in many goods have moved substantially higher. Copper prices rose 41% in 2025. Aluminum, steel, and tin were up 17%, 32%, and 39%, respectively. The costs are likely to flow through to the price of manufactured goods in the coming year, sustaining upward pressure on goods prices.

Food (13.5% of CPI) and energy (6.4%) are highly volatile, which is why economists often focus on the core CPI measurement that excludes them. Nonetheless, they heavily influence headline CPI and shape consumer perceptions of inflation, which can affect discretionary spending. Food prices have been mixed over the past year, trending below their 10-year average, while gasoline prices have declined sharply over the last three years amid a surge in global oil supply.

We expect the oil glut to persist into early 2026. However, if prices fall much further, high-cost producers are likely to cut output quickly while China absorbs floating seaborne inventories. Moreover, predictions of demand destruction driven by electric vehicle adoption appear to have been exaggerated. Without a recession, we believe oil could find and probe a bottom in early 2026 before moving higher — an out-of-consensus view that may surprise markets in the second half.

Putting this together, we see downward pressure on CPI in the near term, with two caveats:

1. Recent seasonal adjustments have tended to add upside pressure to January readings, and

2. Artificially low shelter readings tied to the shutdown could temporarily lift shelter in the second quarter as data collection catches up.

Even so, moderating inflation should be welcome for the Fed, and it may provide cover for an additional rate cut if the labor market remains soft. However, we believe a rate cut would be a mistake: growth should begin accelerating in the spring, and further monetary easing could amplify the second wave of inflation we anticipate later in the year.

Definitions

Absolute return – The return an asset achieves over a specified period of time.

Artificial intelligence ('AI') – A technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity, and autonomy.

Balance of trade, trade balance – The difference between the values of a nation's imports and exports for a defined period of time.

Consolidation – A term used in technical analysis to describe when assets reverse previous gains (or losses) to stay within well-defined trading levels.

Consumer Price Index ('CPI') – Measures the change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics bases the index on prices of food, clothing, shelter, transportation, doctors' and dentists' services, drugs, and other goods and services that people buy for day-to-day living.

Core CPI, the U.S. Consumer Price Index ('CPI') for All Urban Consumers: All Items Less Food & Energy – A measurement of the change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services, compiled by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, which excludes two categories (food and energy) where prices tend to swing up and down more dramatically and more often than other prices.

Correlation – The degree to which two securities move in relation to each other.

Credit spread – The difference in yield between bonds of a similar maturity but with different credit quality. The spread is measured in basis points. Tighter spreads generally suggest that economic prospects are improving and the yield difference decreases as prices rise for riskier bonds that offer higher interest payments than the government.

Credit spread tightening – The contraction of spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause a decline in credit risk.

Cyclical sectors – Sectors that have prices influenced by macroeconomic changes in the economy and are known for following the economy as it cycles through expansion, peak, recession, and recovery.

Demand destruction – A permanent or sustained decline in demand for goods and services as a response to persistent high prices or limited supplies.

Duration – A bond's yield, coupon, final maturity, and call features incorporated into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Effective interest rates – The real percentage rates, adjusted for the effects of inflation, owed in interest on a loan or other debt.

Exchange-traded fund ('ETF') – A type of security that tracks a market index, sector, commodity, or other assets, but which can be bought or sold on a stock exchange the same way a regular stock or other security can. An ETF can be structured to track a wide variety of securities, including stocks, bonds, individual commodities, diverse aggregations of securities, and specific investment strategies.

Federal funds rate — The target interest rate set by the Federal Open Market Committee of the U.S. Federal Reserve. The target is the Fed's suggested rate for commercial banks to borrow and lend their excess reserves to each other overnight.

Fiscal stimulus – Tax collection or government spending aimed at boosting growth in a country's economy.

Gross domestic product ('GDP') – The total value of goods and services provided in an economy during a specified period, often one quarter or one year.

Headwind – A term used to describe events or market forces that hinder the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

High-yield bonds – Bonds that pay higher interest rates because they have lower credit ratings than investment-grade bonds. High-yield bonds have credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor's or below Baa3 from Moody's.

Investment-grade bonds – Fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor's or Baa or better by Moody's.

Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey ('JOLTS') – A program that produces monthly data on job openings, hires, and separations compiled by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. The survey's job openings rates consider month-to-month changes in the number of job openings reported on both a state and national level.

Large language models (LLMs) – Artificial intelligence algorithms that can recognize, summarize, translate, predict, and generate text, as well as respond to questions in a conversational manner, by massively large sets of data. Large language

models learn context and meaning by tracking relationships in sequential data, such as words in a sentence.

Monetary stimulus – Policy decisions made by central banks aimed at boosting growth in a country's economy.

Morningstar Inflation-Protected Bond group – Inflation-protected bond portfolios that primarily invest primarily in debt securities and adjust their principal values in line with the rate of inflation. The U.S. Treasury is currently the largest issuer of these types of securities.

Multiple, price multiple, earnings multiple – A measure of a company's value based on the ratio of its current share price to its earnings per share. This ratio is known as the price-to-earnings ratio, or P/E.

One Big Beautiful Bill Act – An act passed and signed into law in July 2025 that raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, and changed a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, making changes to health insurance legislation, phasing out or reducing credits for clean energy production or use, and removing tax benefits for illegal immigrants.

Overweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Quits rate – The number of quits, or individuals who have left jobs, during the entire month as a percentage of employment. Reported as part of the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey ('JOLTS').

Relative return – the return an asset achieves compared to a benchmark indicator over a specific period.

Secular forces – Large-scale, ongoing changes in economies or societies that have the potential to drive broad and lasting economic, technological, social or other kinds of change.

Tailwind – An event or market force that exerts a positive influence on an investment's performance.

U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ('TIPS') – Investments intended to provide protection against inflation. The principal of a TIPS instrument increases with inflation and decreases with deflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index. When a TIPS matures, investors are paid the adjusted principal or original principal, whichever is greater.

Yield curve – A line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity.

data.title: Midway Through The Robotics Earnings Season: Discipline Rewarded, Recovery Broadening
data.description:
By Zeno Mercer

The current earnings season cycle is delivering a consistent message across the ROBO Index universe: the sector is moving at two speeds, and both are heading in the right direction.

A first cohort of companies with exposure to AI-driven data centers and vision, battery manufacturing, and e-commerce logistics is already reporting strong growth.

A second cohort, still digesting softer general industrial and automotive demand, is doing so with impressive financial discipline and is now positioned to accelerate as those end markets recover.

Buying the Ticket to Recovery

Jenoptik (JNPKF), a German precision photonics and optical systems group, reported preliminary revenue down roughly 6% on semiconductor and automotive weakness, yet held EBITDA margins at 18.4% and grew free cash flow 48% year over year.

Management guided a return to growth on the back of improving semiconductor and life science demand. CFO Dr. Prisca Havranek-Kosicek pointed to “strong growth platforms in semiconductors, life science and medical technology” as the foundation for the recovery.

Meanwhile, Rockwell Automation (ROK), a US industrial automation systems integrator leader, capped its fiscal year with Q4 organic sales growth of 13% and adjusted EPS up 32%, with forward guidance of 3-7% sales growth as AMR production ramps up in Milwaukee. “With our differentiated portfolio and relentless focus on execution, we are well positioned for continued profitable growth,” said Chairman and CEO Blake Moret.

Further evidence that discipline is paying off: Cognex (CGNX), the Massachusetts-based global leader in industrial machine vision, closed the year with adjusted EPS growth of 38%, free cash flow at a multi-year high, and six consecutive quarters of year-over-year EBITDA margin expansion, which is the direct result of a salesforce transformation and AI product investment that accelerated new customer acquisition threefold and the beginning of an overall market recovery.

Already at Full Speed

IPG Photonics (IPGP), a leading developer of high-power fiber lasers for industrial and medical applications, posted its first full-year revenue growth in several years, with Q4 up 17% year over year, medical revenue hitting a record, and book-to-bill finishing firmly above 1.

At the faster end of the sector, Japanese automation leaders are reinforcing the picture. Daifuku (DFKCY), the world’s largest material handling systems provider, raised its full-year revenue forecast and lifted its annual dividend 38%, while Fuji Electric (FELTY), a diversified industrial automation and power electronics group, reported record net sales and operating profit through the first nine months of its fiscal year, driven by energy and industrial automation demand.

What This Means for ROBO

The ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index thesis has always been straightforward: automation is a structural, multi-decade shift. Cyclical softness in capital goods is inevitable.

What distinguishes the long-term compounders is their ability to protect margins through the trough and capitalize when demand returns. The current earnings season cycle is confirming that thesis with specificity.

data.title: What We Can Learn From The Silver Drawdown
data.description:
In early 2000, when the Internet bubble peaked, there were a lot of traders who had entered Nasdaq stocks late in the 1990s because it seemed as though prices would never stop going up (Figure 1).

Figure 1. Nasdaq futures, back-adjusted, 1998-1999. (Data source: CSI)

Of course, we know what happened. At the very beginning of 2000, prices broke. Nasdaq lost more than 80% of its value and declined through 2002, as we can see in Figure 2.

Figure 2. Nasdaq futures, the reaction to the internet bubble. (Data source: CSI)

When the decline started, I remember that many advisors said, “Stay with it; it’s just a correction.” When the index reached 3500 (that would be 7000 on the back-adjusted chart), they said, “We expect prices to stabilize here.” And we can see in Figure 2 that there was a rally after the first break. So they were right, but that was short-lived.

In November 2000, the prices broke down again. “Why get out now? We must be near the bottom.” Finally, in August 2002, NASDAQ had lost 82% of its value.

The question is, "What do you do when the market sells off after a big run-up? We have some recent examples in silver, gold, and even cocoa."

Taking a Rorschach Test

In the following Figures, I want you to find where you would have bought during the drawdown following the run-up. It may be difficult to say where you would have exited from the rally, but in this case, I want you to decide where you would reenter a long position after the drawdown.

We’ll start with a few older cases. Figure 3 shows the 1987 stock market “crash.”

Figure 3. S&P futures, 1987. (Data source: CSI)

There was a lot of money to be made after the crash, or possibly a lot of losses.

We’ve already shown the Nasdaq reaction to the internet bubble in Figure 2. Be honest, where would you have bought the S&P?

The next significant drawdown was the financial crisis of 2008, shown in Figure 4. Would you have bought at 1400? That might have looked good at the time. If you were smart, you placed a stop at the lows.

Figure 4. The financial crisis of 2008. (Data source: CSI)

Perhaps we should even look at 1929. Not a pleasant time. You could have done well buying at 200 and exiting at 300 (Figure 5). If not, you may have lost everything.

It is interesting that the Federal Reserve caused the 1929 crash by continuing to raise interest rates in order to slow speculation. That turns out to be the same scenario that caused the 1987 crash. You would think they would learn. They also failed to act to save the banks, a lesson remembered when the Fed acted in 2008. Thank goodness Bernanke remembered.

Figure 5. Dow Jones, 1928 to 1939. (Data source: CSI)

More Recently

Those were part of history. More recently we had cocoa in 2025, and now gold and silver. I’m avoiding Bitcoin because it has no underlying value, and as such, is unpredictable.

Cocoa had a spectacular run in 2024 and then again the next year. Unlike the previous examples, it has two spikes. Do you know why? Why does it differ from the S&P, Nasdaq, or even gold and silver?

Figure 6. Cash cocoa prices, 2021-2026. (Data source: CSI)

I’ll skip the decline due to Covid-19 and look at gold and silver. Personally, I stopped trading silver 40 years ago because I considered it the “poor man’s gold.” It never had a trend that you could count on, so the recent rally was unexpected (by me). Figure 7 shows cash silver prices.

Figure 7. Cash silver prices, 2025-February 11, 2026. (Data source: CSI)

Is this the bottom for silver? It has a nice bounce at about $70. Will it decline or rally again like cocoa? Of course, you don’t have to guess; you can just wait to see what happens. I find it interesting that the financial news always has a reason why silver can rally. Now it is because of industrial use. I didn’t know it had industrial use until recently. And whatever use it has is going to be offset by a rising price.

And then there is gold, which is much more stable than silver. Gold has been a hedge against the U.S. dollar for a long time, but recent “sell the U.S.” has encouraged traders to buy more gold. While silver has declined 25%, gold has declined 6%. We can see that in Figure 8.

Figure 8. Cash gold prices, 2025 to February 2026. (Data source: CSI)

My Interpretation

A lot of investors would like to think that all crashes have a “V” bottom. While there may be a short “V” rally, it doesn’t last long. Ultimately, large run-ups have large declines and a slow recovery.

Cocoa is different from the financial markets. Once there is a crop failure, it can take two or three years to replenish the crop. That accounts for the second rally in Figure 6. It might have gone into a third year under different circumstances.

When I was a partner in an Illinois farming operation, we always looked at the “carry-over” stocks to see if prices the next year were dependent on the new crop. It’s part of knowing the business.

The financial markets aren’t that way. But investors tend to jump on a rally and overshoot what is probably the right price. Or, in the case of silver or even cocoa, consumers find an alternative at a lower price. It’s economics.

data.title: Housing Starts Up 6.2% In December, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Housing starts rose 6.2% in December to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.404 million, exceeding the forecast of 1.310 million. This represents a -7.3% decline compared to one year ago.

Background on Housing Starts

Housing starts track how many residential buildings began construction in the preceding month. The data is divided into three types of structures: single-family homes, residences with 2-4 units (condos or townhouses), and structures with 5+ units (apartment complexes). A critical aspect of the home-building industry is the powerful influence it has on the rest of the economy.

Here is the historical series for total privately owned housing starts, which dates from 1959. Because of the extreme volatility of the monthly data points, a six-month moving average has been included.

Housing Starts: Structure Breakdown

In December, single-family housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.981 million, the highest level in ten months. This represents a 4.1% increase from November but a -9.0% drop from one year ago.

Multi-family building housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.402 million. This represents a 10.1% increase from November but a -1.0% decline from one year ago.

Note: Buildings with 2-4 units only have N.S.A. data available because tests for identifiable and stable seasonality do not meet reliability standards.

Housing Starts: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Here is the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show substantial growth in the US population since 1959. Here is a chart of housing starts as a percent of the population. We've added a linear regression through the monthly data to highlight the trend.

Housing Starts: A Footnote on Volatility

The extreme volatility of this monthly indicator is the rationale for paying more attention to its six-month moving average than to its noisy monthly change. Over the complete data series, the absolute month-over-month average percent change was 6.3%. The month-over-month range minimum was -26.4%, and the maximum was 29.3%.

For visual confirmation of the volatility, here is a snapshot of the monthly percent change since 1960.

A Long-Term Look: Residential Building Permits and Housing Starts

In addition to housing starts, the U.S. Census Bureau and the Department of Housing and Urban Development published their findings for new residential building permits. Building permits typically preceded housing starts, offering a glimpse into future construction activity.

Despite the fact that both are monthly seasonally adjusted annualized rate (SAAR) series, they are exceptionally volatile and subject to extensive revisions. Thus it is unwise to assign much significance to a single month.

Over the long haul, however, the two offer a compelling study of trends in residential real estate, especially when we adjust the permits and starts for population growth. Here is an overlay of the two series since the 1959 inception of the starts data and the 1960 inception of the permits data.

The monthly data points are preserved as faint dots. The trends are illustrated with six-month moving averages of data divided by the Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates.

Here is a closer look at the overlay since 1990.

ETFs associated with home builders include Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).
