data.title: JXI: Three Reasons I Like This Utilities Fund For The Current Environment
data.description:
Main Thesis/Background

The purpose of this article is to discuss the iShares Global Utilities ETF (JXI) as an investment option at its current market price. This is my first review of this fund and has come about because I continue to look for ways to diversify and add to my non-US and non-Tech allocation. The Utilities space has been a natural diversifier for me over time, as my followers are aware. Given its light weighting in the S&P 500 and income focus, it makes for a perfect supplement to my other holdings.

JXI in particular has been top-of-mind of late for a few reasons. Chief among them is performance. This ETF has soared over the past year and has beat out other Utility-focused funds I follow (and own):

With such a strong gain, it could be easy to say now is not a "good time" to be adding to JXI (or the other utility funds, for that matter). But after review, I would take the counterargument. I continue to see tailwinds that will drive global utility shares higher, and I think JXI is a fine way to play that theme. For this reason I am considering opening up a position in this ETF, and I will use this article to explain my reasoning.

I want to note that I own the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI) and the iShares US Utilities ETF (IDU), which were used for comparison in the 1-year chart.

I Want the Non-US Exposure, Mainly in Europe

One of the primary reasons JXI is on my radar has to do with the fact it has non-US holdings within the Utilities sector. This makes it different than many of the passive ETFs on the market for US retail investors, one of whom I am myself. While the iShares US Utilities ETF (IDU), which I own, and other similar funds such as the Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF (VPU) and the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) hold US companies exclusively, JXI is different. It is "US light" so to speak, with about 40% of its holdings in other regions.

I actually find this attractive because Europe is seeing similar trends in terms of data center electricity demands as we are here in the US. As AI dominates the conversation in the developed world, countries are aggressively trying to keep their capacity up-to-date with the demand. Businesses are consuming more and more electricity in both the US and Europe, and that trend is anticipated to continue well into the next decade (and probably longer):

The simple conclusion here is that I see value in capturing this upside demand for the Utilities sector on a global scale. Yes, this theme is perhaps more pronounced in the US, and the US-focused Utilities ETF has ridden higher as a result. But Europe is in on the trend too, so finding a fund (like JXI) that owns both is kind of a win-win in my mind. This is why I think a "buy" rating is appropriate despite the run it has already had over the past year. This demand story is forecasted to be multi-year, which tells me the gains are not over.

I Like It Over BUI at the Moment

Those who have followed my work for some time might wonder how this is different than BUI. This is a CEF I have championed on many occasions, and it has non-US exposure within the Utilities space. In fact, its US weighting is around 60% as well, so why not just keep on with this option going forward?

The answer is two-fold. One, BUI has a higher share of Canadian companies at the expense of the European exposure. This means JXI fits my "Europe play" a bit better and also helps with diversification more broadly:

For the reasons I noted in the prior paragraph, I like the European slant for the time being. So JXI looks more attractive in a side-by-side comparison.

The second reason has to do with the valuation story. Being an actively managed CEF, BUI can trade above or below its net asset value (NAV). This makes it different from JXI and the other US-oriented ETFs in this space.

This isn't inherently "bad." In fact, it can be a good thing when investors can tactically add at discounted prices. The problem right now is a discount isn't what BUI has. It currently has a premium to NAV on the open market at just over 5%. This makes me reluctant to add to the fund, as I prefer to get new positions closer to par or at a discount. So JXI will have to do at this time while I patiently collect BUI's distributions and hope the valuation becomes more favorable in the future.

Energy Needs Continue to Shift

My third rationale here speaks to the electrification of the developed world's power grids. This is certainly something we are seeing in the US and Europe, and other nations are going to catch on in the future as well. We are seeing power demand coming from a number of different sources, so this is a more diversified field than it was decades ago. From trends like electric vehicles to data centers and wind farms, world trade with respect to energy needs is evolving at a rapid pace.

The reality is this has led to lots of new investment in this space, and Utilities has become a leader in the category. It has changed from a "boring" sector when I started investing in it back in 2008 to a dynamic and forward-thinking sector that has delivered much stronger returns over time than I had anticipated.

The relevance here is multifold. First, JXI fits the bill quite nicely here, as it is heavily exposed to the Electric Utilities sub-sector within this space. With over 60% of its holdings allocated to this area, this ETF will perform well as long as the aforementioned trends continue (which they will):

When I say this is an area at the "forefront" I really do mean it. Investment in this space has been roaring higher. This can be a challenge, as the underlying companies in this space (the utility providers) are doing the investment. This can pressure margins and profits if the investments don't work out as planned:

But I am not as concerned about that risk. Governments are putting a lot of emphasis on electric demand now and for the future. This should lend itself to a more favorable regulatory environment where rate hike requests (by the providers) are approved — likely more generously than in the past. This will help the Utilities sector recoup some of this investment via higher rates to its corporate and household customers.

Risk: Not Much is Cheap, Including Utilities

I have painted this fund with a bullish brush, and I do stand by that call. But that is not to say there is no risk here. Clearly, after a 30% pop higher (in just one year), investors should approach this thesis with some level of caution. Do I think it will move higher throughout 2026? Yes, I do. But I'm not blind to the potential of a correction right around the corner.

What I mainly have concerns with for the time being is valuation. This may seem natural given the rapid rise in JXI's share price, and that is indeed part of the reason. But beyond just being "more expensive" in terms of absolute price, when we look relative to the Utilities sector own trading history, we see that current levels are near the top end of the range:

Now, the good news is this graphic does not suggest the sector is wildly overpriced. But it sure doesn't show "value" either. Sectors do have a way of reverting to their mean, although the range can shift over time, and I believe that is part of what we are seeing today. I think this sector is going to normalize in a higher P/E range because it is simply not the same sector it used to be. It is no longer as stable and income-driven but rather one that is full of investment and new opportunity. That can excite investors and sustain a more elevated valuation story.

But the reverse case can also be true. Utilities, including JXI and many other funds, have had a great run, and a breather could be in the cards. With the sector above its 20-year average in terms of valuation, a pullback could be driven by some profit-taking, and that could lead to some short-term volatility and pressure turns if one initiates new positions today. Keep this in mind and consider your own tolerance for volatility when evaluating the sector's potential going forward.

Bottom Line

The Utilities sector has a bright future ahead, and I believe JXI will be a primary beneficiary of the trends that have been pushing it higher. The international element also allows US-focused investors like myself to add to this space without being over-concentrated in just one region of the world. While I have used BUI in the past for this strategy, that fund's premium to NAV has me looking elsewhere, and I see JXI filling the void nicely. I am therefore initiating my coverage on this ETF with a "buy" rating and would suggest my followers give the idea some consideration at this time.

data.title: SaaS Apocalypse: The Law Of The Strongest Crushing The Weak
data.description:
Technology is Advancing Very Quickly

When ChatGPT was released at the end of 2022, I believe that few people would have projected 800 million weekly active users by early 2026; Gemini passed 750 million active users. Often, investors tend to compare the current technological phase to the dotcom era, but there is a crucial aspect that is typically put on the back burner: AI is spreading much faster across the globe.

It took decades to get a widespread adoption of the internet; for AI, it is taking much less. A couple of weeks ago, as a joke, I showed Gemini to my father (the least tech-savvy person in the world) just to prove to him that AI knows the answer to almost every question. I thought that conversation would have ended there, but surprisingly, he decided to download the app. To date, he still doesn’t know how to send an email, but he uses Gemini on a daily basis. It took him a couple of decades to start using the internet for the first time since its invention, but just three years to start using AI. Would I have ever thought this back in 2022? Absolutely not.

This is just an anecdote, but I think it is very useful to summarize my main vision about the current technological landscape: we don’t know what the world will look like in a couple of years. What we are living through has no precedent, and this statement is supported by objective data.

Just 4 companies combined (Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet), through their huge CapEx, are supporting the entire US GDP growth. In 2026, they are expected to spend $700 billion on AI investments. To put this figure in perspective, it would be comparable to the entire GDP of countries like Singapore (AAA rated), Austria, and the United Arab Emirates. Not small economies.

They all generate a huge amount of cash flow from operations, yet they are issuing bonds worth tens of billions of dollars. No amount of money seems enough to match the demand for this new technology. Of course, the market is becoming doubtful about all these expenses, but since they are all growing by double digits despite their trillion-dollar size, it's difficult to disagree with them.

At this point someone might argue that what we are experiencing right now has already happened multiple times, and the ending has always been the same: a bubble burst.

Technology Era

Peak Investment (% of US GDP)

Duration

Railroads (UK/US)

~6.0%

1840s (UK) / 1870s (US)

Electricity

~2.0%

Early 1920s

Dot-com

~1.5% – 2.0%

1997–2000

AI

~0.8% – 1.5%

2023–

It happened with railroads, electricity, and the internet, and of course, it might happen with AI as well. However, there is one significant difference: back then, the entire industry was not driven mainly by just four companies. There were thousands of them funded by IPOs competing with each other for a share of the market. Today as well we have thousands of AI companies, but no one comes even close to the spending capacity of the big four. They always arrive first.

The AI bubble might burst tomorrow, and the many Palantirs and Teslas will sink; however, META/AMZN/GOOG/MSFT won’t go anywhere. Their huge investments are funded by real profits thanks to their global competitive advantage. History has never seen such a dominant business, and it doesn’t surprise me that GOOG’s century bond recently issued has been 10x oversubscribed; I wonder if a century Treasury bond would have had the same demand.

Another main difference with the previous technology eras is that the AI fruition is much faster than the previous ones. Even before the AI boost, the Big Four could already rely on billions of customers that constantly used their apps, so every innovation could be spread across the globe at the speed of light. You don’t have to build the rail tracks; the internet infrastructure is already there. Sure, these companies are speeding up the process to expand data centers, but the worldwide AI adoption is happening very fast anyway.

The point is, what took 70 years, maybe 150 years for the Industrial Revolution, may happen in 20 years, 25 years. I would love to compress what happened in 200 years of the Industrial Revolution into a 20-year period, if we’re lucky.

Satya Nadella, Microsoft CEO.

If back in 2000 we didn’t know who the winners would have been, today we definitely have some more clues, and their valuations are not even that expensive. Except for GOOG, they are all traded at a Price/CFO below 20x. Don’t look at the free cash flow; their CapEx makes it misleading.

In light of these considerations, it is clear that I think these companies are going to dominate the world over the next decades, but this comes at a significant cost: many other companies won’t survive. It is the law of the strongest that crushes the weak, and in this case, the weak are almost every company on the planet. Amazon projects $200 billion in capex in 2026, and it might be even higher in 2027. If you count the total listed companies in the world, fewer than 100 have a market cap of $200 billion. In other words, the big four have almost an unlimited spending capacity that could potentially wipe out whoever goes against them: SaaS companies are not excluded.

Who is at the Bottom of the Food Chain?

If companies were predators, I would say that Meta, Google, Alphabet, and Amazon are at the peak of the food chain; all the others are at least a step below (OpenAI, Anthropic), and some of them are at the bottom. Now, the goal of every investor should be understanding in which category his/her companies fit in. To help you in this challenging task, I provide you three main questions that you should ask yourself.

  • #1 Monetization test: Does the business model charge for outcomes or total users? A company that benefits from the growth of the workforce might suffer in the current economic context. AI is supposed to reduce the workforce and improve efficiency; therefore, instead of 5 users paying for the same subscription, in the future, companies might need just 2 users to get the same outcome. That’s the core aspect behind the AI revolution.
  • #2 Proprietary test: Does the company own the data? As you know, AI requires huge databases to train on, and some of them are privately held. LLMs can provide good answers just by using public data, but an LLM that trains on private data can be much more valuable. Some niches can’t rely on public data, as they are sometimes misleading or not profound enough. LLM companies must pay the ones that own them.
  • #3 Switching cost test: How difficult is it to switch platforms? Over the next years, some companies might present the best technology in a specific industry, but that doesn’t mean that they will be the winner. Sometimes, industries are protected by laws, incentives, and an unwillingness to take responsibility. Think about it, if you were a manager of an important company that has been using the same software for decades (and it still works pretty well), would you take the responsibility of making a historic change, even if the alternative looks better? What if something goes wrong? I guess you don’t want to risk your job unless there is a big incentive to do it.

If the company in your portfolio has at least one of these advantages (business model per outcome, owns the data, high switching cost), I don’t think AI will destroy it. However, it might change radically, and this means significant headwinds ahead. For example, let’s consider Salesforce (CRM) and FactSet (FDS), two companies that I have analyzed multiple times. They are both crashing, apparently for no fundamental reason since EPS is at an all-time high. So what’s wrong with these companies? They don’t comply with the three characteristics aforementioned.

Both Salesforce and FactSet charge a subscription per user; therefore, AI seems counterproductive for their business model. Of course, they are trying to introduce AI in their platform in order to enhance it, but the market is not buying this narrative. A couple of months ago I wasn’t worried at all about their moat, but the release of Claude Cowork and Claude Code in early February by Anthropic (Nvidia, Microsoft, and Alphabet have invested in it) is reshaping my convictions. I might have underestimated how fast the technological landscape is moving.

Claude Cowork and Claude Code are no longer just chatbots; in fact, they are approaching human efficiency. They can execute multiple tasks across files, review and correct their own mistakes, provide legal and financial analysis outcomes, as well as marketing and data analytics: the AGI gets closer. And these models haven't reached their efficiency peak yet, as more updates will come over the next months/years. In this fierce competition, the big four are not standing still as they are investing every year the amount of entire GDPs of developed countries. Meanwhile, FactSet and Salesforce combined don’t even spend $1 billion in CapEx.

It is a very difficult situation to evaluate, as I think that not even AI experts really know what is coming next. Could it be that Claude (or whatever AI agent) is close to making the same financial analysis of FactSet, and companies no longer need to pay thousands of dollars in subscriptions? I don’t know; it might be. Could it be that spending trillions of dollars in CapEx will make the Salesforce platform no longer the most efficient CRM? I can’t exclude it anymore. However, what I exclude is that these companies are not going to zero.

Salesforce’s main problem is just the #1 characteristic, but the #2 and #3 are pretty solid right now. Remember the previous talk of the manager who doesn’t take responsibility for changing? Well, Salesforce perfectly fits in this description. Its CRM is rooted in thousands of companies with thousands of employees and billions of data: it would be a nightmare to abandon Salesforce. The switching costs are high, and even if there is a better platform available, I am not sure that managers would make such a risky change. After all, the current Salesforce platform works pretty well.

However, even though it is solid, the company needs to restructure its business model, and it will not be the only one. FactSet passes with flying colors only the Proprietary test #2; in fact, it holds decades of high-value data from thousands of companies. Maybe, in the future (or even now), Claude will be able to provide a financial analysis of the same quality, but it first has to pay for FactSet’s database.

Final Thoughts

Overall, I don’t think that AI will bankrupt every SaaS company, but most of them will face a turning point. What has worked in the past might not work in the future, and since the technological innovations are so fast, I decided to sell both companies in early January (lucky timing in hindsight).

This doesn’t mean that Salesforce and FactSet can’t generate significant returns; it just means that these two businesses now present a risk/reward ratio that no longer fits my risk tolerance. I wanted to stick just with the companies that I believe will be the clear winners, therefore Google and Meta. Plus, I added some companies that are facing the SaaS apocalypse consequences, but which are actually only slightly affected.

For example, FactSet’s drop is spreading fear to all companies that sell financial services, even S&P Global (SPGI). However, the latter has a wide moat that cannot be erased by the latest Claude version. Over 60% of its EBIT relies on credit rating issuance and licensing indexes. The first is protected by legal standards; the second cannot be replaced by better AI indexes: the industry benchmarks are already set, and no one can replace the S&P 500 index or Dow Jones Industrial Average. In other words, I am focusing my attention on the companies that belong to the top of the AI food chain and those that are not even competing for the lead because they don’t need to. What’s in the middle of these two categories goes beyond my comprehension right now.

The biggest risk of the current times is doubling down on a company that had good numbers in the past but belongs to the bottom of the food chain. Basically, a prey, and you end up losing it all. I definitely want to avoid as much as possible getting trapped in one of those companies, even if it means leaving on the table huge profits following a potential turnaround.

The trickiest part is that you don’t know which company is the prey until it becomes obvious, but at that point it is too late. I prefer maintaining a simple approach and avoiding overcomplicating things: I'm on the side of those who are winning or have never been in the game.

data.title: TSLL Vs. TSLS: Trading TSLA Through The Pricing Cycle
data.description:
The Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares ETF (TSLL) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with 200% exposure to Tesla (TSLA) shares; the Bear 1X Shares (TSLS) will provide 1x the inverse performance of TSLA shares.

With growing uncertainty relating to Tesla’s near-term growth trajectory following the elimination of the EV tax credits at the end of September 2025, investors must weigh whether TSLA shares are priced for reentry or if further selling pressure may occur. Given the emerging dynamics, leveraged strategies may be appealing for investors given the relatively high volatility of TSLA shares. Given the risky nature of leveraged strategies, TSLL and TSLS should only be used for daily exposure.

As a disclosure, my most recent coverage of TSLA shares held a Sell rating.

Rationale for TSLA Shares

I’m expecting Tesla to face moderate growth challenges for the coming quarters as EV sales face mounting pressure in the US following the elimination of the EV consumer tax credits. Overall, the EV market faced a sharp decline in Q4'25, with Tesla being no exception.

I’m expecting selling pressure to persist going forward, particularly as peer automotive OEMs write off EV assets to favor traditional ICE and hybrid models. The challenge for Tesla in this narrative is that the firm only produces EVs for the vehicle market, providing little breathing room for a strategic pivot. Rubbing salt in the wound was President Trump’s deregulation of the emissions standards for new vehicles, potentially pushing out EV adoption in favor of ICE and hybrid models.

Despite the challenging market environment for EVs, Tesla is pivoting the business model into other markets that may provide relief from declining EV sales. One of the emerging platforms is Tesla’s robotaxi program, which may challenge the rideshare market with unmanned, unsupervised self-driving vehicles. In the near term, I’m expecting Tesla to experience incremental operating costs to ramp up production of the Cybercab to support the robotaxi program. Over time, I believe costs may smooth out as recurring revenue ramps up in the program.

As part of the robotaxi program, Tesla is in a unique position to provide a closed-loop system for all functions of the vehicle, from production to operations. For example, Tesla can support all functions like the charging network, power production through its solar panel business, and satellite communications through SpaceX Starlink, which may result in margin-accretive growth once scaled.

The other growth driver is the Tesla Optimus humanoid robot program. Tesla announced in January 2025 that factory production lines for the Model S & X will be converted to manufacture Optimus robots. Accordingly, Elon Musk anticipates that Tesla can produce 1mm units per year once the production lines are converted. The question is when Optimus will fully scale and whether adoption will occur.

As a result of these three factors [EV sales slump, robotaxi ramp-up, and Optimus development], I believe Tesla may be undergoing a trough period before growth reemerges. Uneven growth may also create opportunities for active traders, as TSLA shares may be more heavily driven by momentum and expected growth over fundamentals.

Looking at the technicals, TSLA shares appear to be on a downward trajectory, though exhibiting strong price run-ups following sharp declines, presenting opportunities for active traders to manage positions in TSLL and TSLS throughout the price waves. Using Elliott Wave Theory, I believe TSLA shares may be entering another wave down following the 0.618 retracement, which may land shares at around $350/share. Fundamentally, I had a price target set at $360/share in my previous report, now lowered to $355/share based on the updated Q4’25 financials. Given this price target, TSLS could be an appealing trading strategy if TSLA shares were to continue their decline to this price level.

Given the fundamental outlook, I believe it may be too soon to turn bullish on TSLA shares as an investment; however, traders can tactically trade through the technical price waves as investors find direction for TSLA shares. This could create an appealing opportunity for TSLL and TSLS for active traders, trading momentum as shares potentially move towards my price target.

About Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares & Bear 1X Shares ETF

TSLL was launched by Direxion on August 9, 2022. TSLL provides traders with 2x the daily performance of TSLA shares by employing a combination of TSLA shares and TSLA swap derivatives. TSLL has a net expense ratio of 95 bps. TSLL exhibits substantial liquidity with $5.6b in net assets and an average of $1.52b in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

TSLS purely employs swap derivatives in order to gain 1x the daily inverse exposure to TSLA shares. TSLS has a net expense ratio of 94 bps. TSLS exhibits more moderate liquidity with $77mm in net assets with an average trading volume of $340mm in share value.

Swaps are designed to settle at the end of each trading day, providing TSLL with exposure to the daily performance of TSLA shares in exchange for a fixed rate. As a result of the daily settlement, TSLL may experience a daily reset in terms of performance that can result in a compounding effect if held for longer than a single trading day. This makes TSLL a risky asset to hold over time, especially given my price forecast, as negative performance could potentially result in unsalvageable losses.

Additional Risks to Consider for TSLL & TSLS

In addition to compounded risks, TSLL may experience NAV erosion over time that may impact an investor’s initial investment. Given that TSLL and TSLS were designed for daily exposure, investors may be exposed to greater short-term capital gains taxes on top of the net expense ratio associated with the funds.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

TSLL and TSLS can be appealing trading strategies for investors seeking to amplify returns on the technical trading path of TSLA shares. With the expectation of a downward trajectory towards $350-355/share, I believe investors may capitalize through the use of TSLS while gaining exposure to wave retracements with TSLL. Given the risks involved in both strategies and the expected path of TSLA shares, I am recommending TSLL with a Sell rating and TSLS with a Buy rating.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Common And Preferred Shares Diverge In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

In my prior coverage of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), I advocated for an income-focused approach, presenting the pros and cons of selling covered calls against the long common stock position, while also highlighting the appeal of the preferred shares for more conservative investors.

Since then the covered call idea has worked quite well, with JPM shares up in the low single digits over the last four months, indicating that investors could have pocketed substantial option premiums without losing too much upside. Since JPM maintains a Seeking Alpha valuation grade of D and trades at a somewhat elevated 14x forward P/E multiple, I think covered calls continue to offer the best risk-reward potential.

That said, since analysts have moved their 12-month price targets to roughly $343/share, up from circa $325/share when I last covered JPM, I would also highlight the increased appeal of selling out-of-the-money covered calls. Of course, investors prioritizing income can continue to sell at-the-money covered calls with a strike price around the current share price of $303/share, which remains my personal preference given my Hold rating on JPM common stock.

I confirm my previous Buy rating on the Series JJ (JPM.PR.J), LL (JPM.PR.L), KK (JPM.PR.K), and MM (JPM.PR.M) preferred shares, as I estimate these preferreds can deliver a total return close to 10% as long as the Fed brings down the Fed funds rate to 3% by year-end 2026, in line with current market pricing.

In contrast, I rate the Series EE (JPM.PR.C) and DD (JPM.PR.D) preferred shares with a Hold rating, as they offer no capital gain potential since they trade at par post their redemption dates.

Income Potential With Covered Calls

The strike price chosen when selling JPM covered calls will naturally depend on the investor's outlook for JPM stock. Personally, I would rate JPM a Hold due to a somewhat elevated valuation and limited room to improve underlying profitability (as it is already at very robust levels, with 2025 RoTCE at 20%).

As such, I view at the money covered calls as the best risk-reward in this case. The March 2026 $305 strike price calls trade at about $9.90/contract, or a monthly option premium yield somewhat above 3%.

While selling monthly covered calls will provide the highest annualized option premium yield, it also brings the lowest near-term protection (if JPM stock suffers a sizable correction). Hence, more conservative investors may want to consider the 3-month May 2026 $305 strike price calls, which trade around $16.00/contract, or a 3-month yield marginally above 5%.

Investors who are more bullish on JPM stock may want to consider selling out of the money covered calls, preferably with a strike price between $325 and $345/share, allowing them to capture a good portion of the 12-month JPM price target of $343/share. For instance, the May 2026 $345 strike price calls trade at about $3.90/share, providing a 3-month yield marginally above 1%.

Selling out of the money covered calls will also allow investors to capture more of JPM's common stock dividends, which stand at $1.50/share per quarter, or a quarterly yield of about 0.5%.

Source of return/strike price At the money options (circa $305/share) Out of the money options (circa $345/share)
Annualized option premium yield High to very high with near-term options Moderate
Dividend yield Very time-dependent You will likely be able to collect some quarterly dividend distributions
Capital gain potential Very low to non-existent Moderate, depending on share price performance

Source: Author's table

As you can tell from the table above, depending on your individual preferences and JPM stock outlook, you can tailor the selling of covered calls to account for factors such as the collection of dividends and the potential for capital gain.

With JPM shares down in the mid-single digits year-to-date, the appeal of covered call selling to mitigate capital losses is also noteworthy. As a rule of thumb, at-the-money options provide larger protection against losses, while out-of-the-money options provide more limited loss protection.

The Preferred Shares

In contrast to the small decline in JPM common stock, JPM's preferred shares have delivered gains in 2026, benefiting from a decline in risk-free long-term interest rates. Even so, the overall performance of my Buy rating issued four months ago has been subpar, as yields are fractionally higher relative to October 2025.

Preferred dividends remain well-covered. JPM paid $335 million in Q4 2025 preferred dividends, a coverage of 36.7x relative to the bank's $13 billion in net income before preferred distributions. JPM has a cumulative $20 billion in preferred equity outstanding, indicating that the $816 billion in common equity market capitalization provides a 40.8x coverage of preferred equity.

With Fed funds futures pointing to a Fed funds rate of about 3% at the end of 2026, I believe long-term interest rates should drop further by year-end, perhaps as much as 0.2-0.25%, providing a tailwind for JPM preferred shares which trade well below par value, as summarized in the table below:

Preferred Series Coupon Current yield Premium/Discount to par Rating
Series EE (JPM.PR.C) 6.00% 5.96% +0.6% Hold
Series DD (JPM.PR.D) 5.75% 5.75% 0% Hold
Series JJ (JPM.PR.J) 4.75% 5.81% -22.3% Buy
Series LL (JPM.PR.L) 4.625% 5.83% -20.6% Buy
Series KK (JPM.PR.K) 4.55% 5.81% -21.7% Buy
Series MM (JPM.PR.M) 4.20% 5.62% -25.3% Buy

Source: Author calculations

Looking at the table above, we see that the Series JJ, LL, KK, and MM preferred shares all offer a discount to par in excess of 20%, further increasing the safety profile of preferred distributions. Given my expectations for a decline in risk-free interest rates and JPM's exceptional preferred dividend coverage, I rate these preferred series with a Buy rating.

In contrast, the Series EE and DD preferred shares are currently eligible to be called at par, indicating no capital gain potential. As such, I only rate them a Hold.

To conclude, I believe investors not willing to take the risk with JPM's common stock and the covered call strategy I outlined above should stick with JPM's preferred shares trading below par, as they will potentially deliver some capital gains on top of already attractive current dividend yields.

To illustrate the capital gain potential, in the table below I have compiled a fair value price target if we see yields on the preferred shares dip by 0.25%:

Preferred Series Target price if yields drop by 0.25% Capital gain potential
Series JJ (JPM.PR.J) $21.4 +4.5%
Series LL (JPM.PR.L) $20.7 +4.4%
Series KK (JPM.PR.K) $20.5 +4.5%
Series MM (JPM.PR.M) $19.55 +4.7%

Source: Author calculations

As you can tell from my calculations, the Series JJ, LL, KK, and MM preferred shares should deliver a circa 4.5% capital gain if we see a parallel 0.25% decline in preferred dividend yields, which is my base case when the Fed is at its 3% neutral rate. Together with current dividend yields of 5.6-5.8%, this should produce ~10% total return for investors.

Risks

Selling covered calls entails several principal risks. Firstly, while the option premium will compensate some losses, a severe correction in JPM stock, most probably driven by financial or economic turmoil, may result in substantial losses for investors. The silver lining is that investors in such a scenario will still be better off than with a simple vanilla buy-and-hold approach.

Secondly, investors selling covered calls lose liquidity of their position, especially if option prices surge in value due to a rise in the VIX index. In essence, even if JPM stock doesn't move too much, the perception that it will do so in the future may imply having to wait until the option expires. Alternatively, investors can close the position early, albeit at a loss.

I should also note that over the very near term, a company update scheduled for February 23, 2026, may produce some near-term volatility in the shares, especially if longer-term plans not previously communicated with the market are discussed.

Last but not least, with JPM stock down year-to-date and analysts raising their price targets, the likelihood of missing out on capital gains when selling covered calls increases. This can be mitigated to an extent by going for out-of-the-money call options.

With preferred shares, the principal risk to consider is that long-term risk-free interest rates may increase, putting pressure on preferred stock prices even if preferred dividend coverage remains sound from a fundamental perspective.

I should also note that the Fed may cut interest rates less than the market is currently pricing in. Alternatively, even if the Fed does cut interest rates, there is no guarantee that a reduction in near-term interest rates will result in a dip in long-term yields as well. As such, the capital gain component is subject to increased uncertainty.

Conclusion

The fortunes of JPM's common and preferred shares have diverged so far in 2026. While analysts have recently boosted 12-month price targets, JPM common stock is down year-to-date, impacted by an elevated valuation and declining interest rates, which may weigh on its net interest income.

In contrast, JPM's preferred shares, especially those trading well below par value, have posted modest gains this year, helped by lower risk-free interest rates.

While a declining common stock price and higher analyst price targets arguably warrant a tilt toward out-of-the-money covered calls, my preference remains for at-the-money covered calls, which provide the highest current yield and largest downside protection.

JPM's preferred dividend safety remains supreme, potentially indicating that preferred yields should decline as long as the Fed brings its Fed funds rate to around 3% by year-end 2026. I estimate a 0.25% decline in preferred yields should result in a total return of around 10% for the Series JJ, LL, KK, and MM preferred shares, rating them a Buy.

I rate JPM common stock a Hold, alongside the Series EE and DD preferred shares, which offer marginally higher yields but no capital gain potential as they are already trading at par past their potential redemption date.

Thank you for reading.
